summary: DHH argues that the real complexity in modern web services comes not from raw computational demands but from multi-tenancy—serving millions of users from a single system. He contends that modern hardware is powerful enough that any single organization could run on a single server with SQLite, and that the industry's SaaS multi-tenancy paradigm creates unnecessary operational complexity. This insight underpins 37signals' ONCE product line, which sells software as single-tenant products rather than renting them as services. He frames this as a pendulum swing back toward distributed, single-tenant architectures and expresses excitement about the conceptual simplifications it could unlock.
key_points: Modern computers are so fast that virtually no single organization can overwhelm a web service on one server—multi-tenancy is the source of all scaling complexity
SQLite's resurgence for non-embedded use cases reflects a growing interest in single-tenant architectures that sidestep multi-tenancy challenges
37signals' ONCE product line bets on selling software as single-tenant products you run yourself, rather than renting multi-tenant SaaS
The cloud's original promise to make scaling easy never materialized—large multi-tenant systems still require serious operations teams
The shift between centralized services and distributed clients is a recurring pendulum in tech, and revisiting old paradigms is how progress happens
money_quotes: Computers have gotten so ridiculously fast that there is scarcely any organization in the world that can overwhelm a web-based information system running on a single server.
All the complexity and sophistication required to run web services today stem from multi-tenancy.
No multi-tenancy, no intermingling of data, much easier scaling dynamics.
Part of the original cloud pitch was that it would become easy, but that never panned out.
There's no major web-based information system with millions of users that's "easy" to run.
That's what's so alluring about single-tenancy, sqlite, and turning services back into products. The possibility of sidestepping much of this complexity for both development and operations.
Our beloved industry consists of a handful of pendulums that continuously swing back and forth, and distributed clients vs centralized services is one of the biggest.
Progress usually requires revisiting your priors.
key_insight: The vast majority of web service complexity exists to serve multi-tenancy, and single-tenant architectures running on modern hardware could eliminate most of that burden by turning services back into products.
